coredata.dc:title: High-efficient synthesis of straw-derived carbon quantum dots for facilitating methanogenic performance in high-load co-digestion of food waste and excess sludge
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qiang
Zhang, Haoyu
Wang, Haozhen
Zhao, Shenggeng
Li, Yeqing
Feng, Lu
Pan, Junting
Zhou, Hongjun
Xu, Chunming
coredata.dc:description: Anaerobic digestion (AD), as a crucial technology for organic waste resource recovery, faces the challenge of low efficiency in converting high-load organic substance into biogas. In this study, high-load AD system with food waste and excess sludge as co-substrates was constructed. The effect and mechanism of carbon quantum dots (CQD) derived from straw in promoting the performance of AD systems have been studied. The oxidation pretreatment of straw with H2O2 and acetic acid increased the yield of hydrothermal synthesis CQD to approximately 40%. The effect of different CQD on CH4 yield performance was further explored. The cumulative CH4 yield performance of the fermenter was improved after adding CQD. The CQD synthesized from pretreated straw and the nitrogen-doped CQD synthesized using Chlorella as the nitrogen source showed competitive promotion performance, increasing cumulative CH4 yield by 17.71% and 8.87%, respectively. These CQD can effectively accelerate the degradation of dissolved organic matter and thus improve the CH4 yield performance, with the most significant effect of CQD synthesized from pretreated straw. Electrochemical analysis and the correlation analysis between microorganisms and performance parameters showed that these CQD established an electron conductive network to enhance the electron transfer of the system. This well conductive conditions enriched hydrogenotrophic methanogenic (Methanosarcina), electroactive bacteria (Clostridium_sensu_stricto_1), and hydrolytic-acidifying bacteria (norank_f__Bacteroidetes_vadinHA17). This study significantly enhanced the yield of straw-derived CQD through green methods, and deeply revealed the potential promoting mechanisms of biomass-derived CQD by investigating the correlation between system performance and microorganisms in high-load AD systems.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM images and particle size distribution of (a) CQD (b) NCQD (c) Pre-CQD. The HRTEM images of (d) CQD (e) NCQD (f) Pre-CQD.
High-resolution C1s、N1s and O1s peaks and fitting curves of (a)-(c) CQD, (d)-(f) NCQD, and (g)-(i) Pre-CQD.
CH4 production performance of high-load AD with different CQD. (a) Cumulative CH4 yield, (b) Daily CH4 yield, and (c) CH4 content.
The variation of pH and VFA during high-load AD with different CQD. (a) AD, (b) ADCQD, (c) ADNCQD and (d) ADPre-CQD.
The variation of (a) SCOD and (b) NH4 +-N during high-load AD with different CQD.
3D-EEM spectra of DOM in (a) AD, (b) ADCQD, (c) ADNCQD and (d) ADPre-CQD on the 1st, 5th, and 36th days.
Electrochemical performance of the digestate for (a) CV, (b) EIS, and (c) electric conductivity.
The relative abundance of (a) bacterial c and (b) archaea at genus level.

The correlation network heat map between microorganisms and performance parameters. The color gradient represents the Spearman’ correlation coefficient and was used for pairwise comparison between microorganisms. The relationship between microbial community composition and performance parameters was analyzed by Mantel test. The width of the edge was proportional to the corresponding distance correlation Mantel statistic, while the color of the edges was used to indicate statistical significance (CMP: Cumulative CH4 yield, MC: CH4 content, EC: Electrical conductivity).
Schematic diagram of potential mechanism on different CQD promoting high-load AD.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Anaerobic digestion (AD), as a crucial technology for organic waste resource recovery, faces the challenge of low efficiency in converting high-load organic substance into biogas. In this study, high-load AD system with food waste and excess sludge as co-substrates was constructed. The effect and mechanism of carbon quantum dots (CQD) derived from straw in promoting the performance of AD systems have been studied. The oxidation pretreatment of straw with H2O2 and acetic acid increased the yield of hydrothermal synthesis CQD to approximately 40%. The effect of different CQD on CH4 yield performance was further explored. The cumulative CH4 yield performance of the fermenter was improved after adding CQD. The CQD synthesized from pretreated straw and the nitrogen-doped CQD synthesized using Chlorella as the nitrogen source showed competitive promotion performance, increasing cumulative CH4 yield by 17.71% and 8.87%, respectively. These CQD can effectively accelerate the degradation of dissolved organic matter and thus improve the CH4 yield performance, with the most significant effect of CQD synthesized from pretreated straw. Electrochemical analysis and the correlation analysis between microorganisms and performance parameters showed that these CQD established an electron conductive network to enhance the electron transfer of the system. This well conductive conditions enriched hydrogenotrophic methanogenic (Methanosarcina), electroactive bacteria (Clostridium_sensu_stricto_1), and hydrolytic-acidifying bacteria (norank_f__Bacteroidetes_vadinHA17). This study significantly enhanced the yield of straw-derived CQD through green methods, and deeply revealed the potential promoting mechanisms of biomass-derived CQD by investigating the correlation between system performance and microorganisms in high-load AD systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the acceleration of global industrialization and urbanization, the generation of organic solid waste has risen dramatically [1]. Several investigates indicated that over 2 billion tons of solid waste were generated annually in global, and this statistic was projected to approach 4 billion tons by 2050. Approximately 50% of these solid wastes were organic waste [2,3]. Anaerobic digestion (AD) can convert organic waste into green biomethane and green carbon dioxide. The recycling of green carbon sources holds immense energy and environmental benefits [4].

Currently, AD technology faces challenges in operational stability. The composition ratio of a single substrate will affect the stability [5]. Especially the excess sludge from municipal wastewater treatment plants has an imbalanced C/N ratio. The methanogenesis potential of AD with a single excess sludge is usually limited [6]. Co-digestion of multi-component substrates has been an effective approach to solving this issue. It has been shown that the improvement of conversion efficiency and energy recovery in AD can be achieved by optimizing substrate ratios [7]. Food waste is a high C/N ratio organic carbon source rich in carbohydrates and fats. Therefore, AD of excess sludge and food waste as co-substrate can regulate C/N and improve system pH to increase the efficiency of AD. Zhang et al. conducted fermentation experiments using food waste with garden waste as co-digested substrate. The results showed that the CH4 yield increased by 73.33% for garden waste and kitchen waste co-digestion, compared to single food waste digestion [7].

Providing exogenous conductive materials is another effective approach to enhancing AD performance [8]. The exogenous conductive materials such as activated carbon [9,10], biochar [11], carbon nanotubes [12], and magnetite [13,14] enhanced direct interspecies electron transfer to improve methanogenesis. Carbon materials were representative objective. Carbon quantum dots (CQD), as a novel quasi-zero dimensional carbon nanomaterial, are utilized in biological imaging, photo/electrocatalyst preparation, as well as detection, due to their excellent biocompatibility, high conductivity and large specific surface area [15,16]. At present, CQD have been proven to have the potential to promote the AD system [17,18]. Although CQD have been widely studied, the importance of carbon source selection has been often neglected. China has a large amount of agricultural waste resources, which were rich in C, H, and O elements. The advantages of low cost and easy availability make them an ideal carbon source for synthesizing CQD [19]. However, biomass-derived CQD face the problem of low yield, and their application in high-load AD still needs further exploration.

In this study, CQD were efficiently synthesized used straw as a precursor through a one-step hydrothermal method. A high-load co-substrate digestion system was constructed and the effects of CQD with different characteristics on this system were investigated. The impact of CQD on AD performance was evaluated using cumulative CH4 yield, soluble chemical oxygen demand (SCOD), NH4 +-N, and pH as indicators. The effects of CQD on the electron transfer in AD was explored by electrochemical analysis. The potential effects of CQD with different properties on changes in dissolved organic matter (DOM) composition and microbial community structure was analyzed. Furthermore, the correlation network heat map correlating microbial community structure and environmental factors was constructed in order to deeply reveal the key environmental factors affecting the microbial community structure. This study comprehensively evaluated the effectiveness and potential mechanisms of biomass-derived CQD in high-load AD systems, providing crucial evidence for the application of CQD in real-world AD.

Food waste was originated from the college cafeteria. Excess sludge was obtained from a sewage treatment plant in Shandong, China. The inoculum for AD was obtained from laboratory-scale continuous stirred tank reactors (CSTR). The total solids (TS) and volatile solids (VS) of substrates and inoculum were shown in Table S1.

The substrate was pre-treated before fermentation. Briefly, food waste was pulverized through a multi-functional high-speed grinder to reduce its granularity, and then screened using a 60-mesh sieve. Excess sludge solution was adjusted to a pH 10 using NaOH solution. Then it was put in an oven at 60 °C for 20 h. The purpose of alkaline pretreatment was to effectively decompose the organic matrix in sludge, thereby improving its utilization in AD [19,20]. After treatment, the pH of excess sludge solution and food waste solution was adjusted to 7 using HCl or NaOH solutions. They were then mixed in a 1:1 ratio for co-digestion substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQD with different properties
ce:section.ce:para: Three different properties of CQD were synthesized using straw as a precursor through a one-step bottom-up hydrothermal method. The process is as follows:

Corn straw was pulverized with a high-speed grinder and sieved through a 60-mesh sieve to obtain the straw powder. 2.0 g powder was mixed with deionized water through 20 W ultrasonic treatment. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave for 400 min at 190 °C. After the reaction, the solution was centrifuged, filtered, and dried to obtain straw-derived CQD and labeled as CQD.

Straw and Chlorella sp. with pyrenoid (as a nitrogen source) were hydrothermally reacted in a 2:1 w/w ratio. Following the hydrothermal conditions in the CQD preparation protocol, the product obtained was nitrogen-doped CQD labeled as NCQD.

Straw powder was soaked in a mixture of 30% H2O2 and glacial acetic acid at a volume ratio of 1:2 v/v, thoroughly mixed by ultrasound. Then the mixed solution was incubated at room temperature (25 °C) for 72 h [21]. During this time, apply microwave ultrasound treatment for 30 min every 12 h. Subsequently, following the hydrothermal conditions in the CQD preparation protocol, the product obtained was labeled as Pre-CQD.

The batch AD experiment was conducted in four-sectioned fermenters, with each 3 L reactor (2 L working volume) (Fig. S1). The total load of food waste and excess sludge was 40 g VS L−1, and the single substrate load was 20 g VS L−1. The experimental groups were set as ADCQD (1 g/L CQD), ADNCQD (1 g/L NCQD), ADPre-CQD (1 g/L Pre-CQD), and the control group AD. Before the experiment, N2 was introduced to ensure an anaerobic environment. Then the experiment was conducted in 37 ± 1 °C at 80 rpm. The biogas was collected using 2 L gas bags, and gas composition and content were measured in every day. A 10 mL digestate was sampled from the export for measuring volatile fatty acid (VFA), NH4 +-N, SCOD, and pH, in order to monitor the kinetics of the AD process. The relevant CQD were supplemented with 0.25 g every 15 days.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan) was used to analyze the morphology and size of all CQD. The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI, USA) was used to analyze the elemental composition of all CQD. The structural properties of the sample were analyzed using Raman spectroscopy (Thermo Fischer DXR, USA). The organic element analyzer (EA, Thermo Scientific FlashSmart, USA) was used to determined organic substance element composition.

The volume of biogas was measured using a simple flow meter device. The content of CH4 were analyzed by Fuli GC9790 Plus gas chromatograph with thermal conductivity detector (TDX-01). The content of VFA was determined by Aglient 7890B gas chromatograph with FID. The content of NH4 +-N and SCOD was determined by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hach DR6000, USA) referring to the APHA method [22]. The pH of the digestion was measured by pH meter (FivEasy Plus, Switzerland). Three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (3D-EEM) spectroscopy was employed to analyze the DOM. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the digestion were determined via an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Chenhua Inc, Shanghai China) according to the previous method [17]. The conductivity of the digestion was determined using a conductivity meter (FE30, China).

The substrates collected from each reactor on the 40th day were tested for microbial community analysis using Illumina MiSeq sequencing [23]. In brief, genomic DNA was extracted using 1% agarose gel electrophoresis. Next, PCR (ABI GeneAmp 9700, USA) was used to amplify the V3-V4 region of the 16S rRNA gene with specific primers 338F (5′-ACTCCTACGGGGAGGCAGC AG-3′) and 806R (5′-GGACTACHVGGGTWTCTAAT3′). After amplification, the product was purified, quantified, and homogenized. Subsequently, the samples were subjected to Illumina high-throughput sequencing.

The modified Gompertz model was used to describe the methanogenesis process, and its equation was expressed as follows:

Where, P(t) is cumulative CH4 yield (mL/g VS), Pmax is maximum CH4 yield potential (mL/g VS), rm is maximum CH4 yield rate (mL/g VS/day), λ is lag time (days), t is the run time (days).

Statistical analyses were conducted using RStudio (version 4.3.3). Unless otherwise specified, the Student t-test was used, with a significance level of p < 0.05 considered statistically significant. The data statistics and presentation software used in this work were OriginPro 2021 (Origin Lab, US).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical properties of different CQD
ce:section.ce:para: After pre-treating the straw powder with the green agents H2O2 and acetic acid, the yield of CQD through hydrothermal treatment reached 40.46 %, which was an increase of nearly 18 % compared to the without pre-treatment. This improvement may be attributed to the decomposition of complex structures such as cellulose and lignin within the straw. This method reduced the consumption of strong acids and bases. A green and efficient pathway for synthesizing CQD using biomass as a precursor was provided. All these CQD displayed excellent dispersibility and solubility in aqueous solution (Fig. 1 ). The average particle size of CQD was effectively reduced due to straw milling, ultrasonication and oxidation treatments. Pre-CQD had the smallest average particle size (2.3 ± 0.02 nm), followed by NCQD (2.6 ± 0.03 nm) and CQD (2.9 ± 1.6 nm) (Fig. 1a-c). The lattice spacing of all CQD were obtained from TEM images. The lattice spacings of Pre-CQD, NCQD, and CQD were 0.188 nm, 0.205 nm, and 0.217 nm, respectively (Fig. 1d-f). These small-sized quantum dots could effectively adhere to microbial surfaces and have the potential to stimulate cellular activity after absorption [24,25].

Three types of CQD exhibited significant characteristic peaks as shown in Fig. S2. The appearance of the N1s characteristic peak indicated that nitrogen has been doped into the CQD. The introduction of Chlorella sp. and pretreatment both increased the nitrogen content of the products, reaching 5.39 % and 4.17 %, respectively (Table S2). After fitting the peaks of C1s, three high-resolution peaks of C-C (284.8 eV/284.8 eV/284.8 eV), C-N (286.1 eV/286.3 eV/286.1 eV), and C=O (288.6 eV/288.4 eV/288.6 eV) were identified (Fig. 2 a, d and g) [26]. Three high-resolution peaks of pyrrole N (399.9 eV/400.1 eV/400.0 eV), pyridine N (399.2 eV/398.3 eV/399.2 eV), and graphite N (401.5 eV/401.4 eV/401.6 eV) were obtained after fitting the N1s peaks (Fig. 2b, e and h) [27]. Nitrogen was mainly present as pyrrole N and pyridine N when Chlorella sp. was doped as a nitrogen source. In particular, the enhanced intensity of the pyrrole N peak and integral content in NCQD confirmed the successful doping of nitrogen (Fig. 2e and Table S3). After fitting the peaks of O1s, three high-resolution peaks, C-O-H (533.8 eV/533.4 eV/533.5 eV), C-O-C (531.5 eV/531.6 eV/531.8 eV), and C=O (532.6 eV/532.5 eV/532.6 eV) were identified (Fig. 2c, f and i) [28]. Specifically, the intensities of the C-O-C (531.8 eV) and C-O-H (533.5 eV) peaks were enhanced (Fig. 2i) compared to the unpretreated samples. In addition, the integral content proportion of the C-O-C and C-O-H peaks increased to 22.70% and 37.06% after pretreatment (Table S3). The C-O-C and C-O-H functional groups, as electron shuttles, could effectively enhance electron transfer capability [24].

In addition, the doping of the nitrogen source and pretreatment improved the graphitization degree of the products, although not significantly. Raman spectroscopy showed (Fig. S3) that the intensity ratios of the D peak to the G peak for NCQD and Pre-CQD were 0.78 and 0.81, respectively, both of which were lower than CQD. The superior graphitization degree indicated higher potential for electronic transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different CQD on high-load AD performance

ce:section.ce:para: During the 43 day experimental period, the cumulative CH4 yield, daily CH4 yield, and daily CH4 content were measured to evaluate the performance of high-load AD. The ADPre-CQD group possessed the highest cumulative CH4 yield of 310.83 mL/g VS, followed by ADNCQD and ADCQD with cumulative CH4 yield of 287.50 mL/g VS and 279.69 mL/g VS (Fig. 3 a). The cumulative CH4 yield of three experimental groups increased by 17.71 %, 8.87 % and 5.92 %, respectively, compared to the control AD (264.07 mL/g VS). In conclusion, the addition of different CQD increased CH4 yield. A recently proposed theory indicated that this enhancement may be attributed to the binding of CQD with microbial conductive pili and c-type cytochromes, which could facilitate electron transfer process [24]. It was worth noting that the effect of Pre-CQD was particularly significant. The smaller particle size of Pre-CQD enabled them to more effectively penetrate cell membranes and thereby promoted intracellular reactions. Additionally, functional groups on the surface of Pre-CQD, such as C-O-C and C-O-H, were potential reasons for further enhancing AD performance.

In the first 5 days, the daily CH4 yield of the control and experimental groups showed an increasing trend, and the peak of daily CH4 yield appeared on the 4th or 6th day (Fig. 3b). The daily CH4 yield of all three experimental groups were higher than that of the control group, and the first CH4 yield peak was advanced by 1–2 days. This result indicated that the addition of CQD significantly promoted methane conversion rate, leading to the earlier appearance of the peak of daily CH4 yield. Subsequently, the daily CH4 yield of the control group and the experimental group showed a decreasing trend. On the 27th day, the daily CH4 yield stabilized and ended on the 43rd day. In addition, the control group exhibited only two peaks in daily CH4 yield, whereas AD with Pre-CQD and NCQD showed three peaks in the entire AD process. The appearance of more daily CH4 yield peaks indicated that the addition of CQD effectively enhanced methanogenesis in the system. Daily CH4 content showed a similar trend to daily CH4 yield (Fig. 3c).

The results of CH4 proportion (Fig. 3c) showed that the daily CH4 content of AD, ADCQD, ADNCQD, and ADPre-CQD reached 62.32 %, 64.57 %, 65.00 % and 65.03 % respectively on the 5th day. Subsequently, from day 5 to day 26, the daily CH4 content of all anaerobic fermenters remained stable. Different types of quantum dots increased the average CH4 content in the systems. Especially NCQD and Pre-NCQD increased 9.61 % and 9.78 % respectively compared to AD.

The analysis of methanogenic kinetics was performed using the modified Gompertz equation (Table S4 and Fig. S4). The R2 values of the Gompertz model for all groups were more than 0.99, which indicated an excellent model fit. The analysis results showed that the highest maximum CH4 yield (Pmax) of 310.22 ± 1.13 mL/g VS was recorded in the ADPre-CQD group, and the Pmax of 286.32 ± 1.32 mL/g VS, 273.52 ± 1.59 mL/g VS, and 263.63 ± 1.40 mL/g VS were recorded in the ADNCQD group, the ADCQD group, and the AD group, respectively. The maximum CH4 rate showed the same trend, with ADPre-CQD group (21.92 ± 0.41 mL/g VS) >ADNCQD group (20.83 ± 0.43 mL/g VS) >ADCQD group (19.68 ± 0.60 mL/g VS) >AD group (16.94 ± 0.42 mL/g) VS). The observation results of the experimental groups producing more CH4 in Fig. 3 was consistent with the fitting results.

During the acidogenesis phase of AD, the organic substrates were decomposed into simpler organic molecules such as VFA by acidogenic bacteria [29,30]. pH and VFA concentrations were critical factors affecting AD performance. As shown in Fig. 4 , the pH value rapidly decreases due to the conversion of substances such as carbohydrates into VFA, mainly acetic acid and propionic acid, at the beginning of AD. The pH of the control and AD with different CQD were maintained at around 6.7 during the first 2 days. After 3 days, the pH gradually increased and reached a dynamic equilibrium between 7 and 8. The optimal pH for most methanogen was between 6.8 and 7.5 [31]. Indicating that the system was not affected by acid inhibition. The pH of the ADPre-CQD group increased rapidly to around 8 on the 3th day (Fig. 4d), while the AD group pH increased the slowest (Fig. 4a), indicating a slower consumption of organic acids, which in turn had an impact on CH4 yield. These findings indicated that the system was not affected by acid inhibition, even when operating under high loads. The pH in the ADPre-CQD group rapidly increased to approximately 8 on the 3th day (Fig. 4d), whereas the pH increase was slowest in all AD group (Fig. 4a). The consumption of organic acids in the control group was unsatisfactory, which limits CH4 production.

The main components of volatile fatty acids were acetic acid and propionic acid, as well as small amounts of butyric acid and isobutyric acid, as shown in Fig. 4. Acetic and propionic acids were subsequently utilized by methanogens for conversion to CH4 [32]. On the 1st and 3rd days, the AD group accumulated 4479.3 mg/L and 4352.1 mg/L of VFA, respectively (Fig. 4a). The performance on the 3rd day was particularly remarkable, being 2.6, 0.11, and 1.5 times higher than the ADCQD, ADNCQD, and ADPre-CQD, respectively. This was due to the slow consumption of VFA by methanogen, as well as the accumulation of acids decreasing metabolic activity [33]. After 5 days, the total VFA content of the experimental groups showed an overall decreasing trend (Fig. 4b-d). By the 17th day, the experimental groups’ total VFA content levels reached a lower point ranging from 123.9 to 181.2 mg/L. At this time, the ADPre-CQD group had a low content of 123.9 mg/L. These results were consistent with our previously observed phenomenon, where a similar inflection point in daily CH4 yield occurred around the 17th day and subsequently stabilized (Fig. 3b). The VFA analysis results indicated that adding different CQD can effectively regulated acid accumulation during high-load co-digestion processes, enhancing the system’s buffering capacity and thereby improving AD performance.

SCOD was a key indicator for assessing the organic carbon content of substrates in high-load AD and was closely correlated with CH4 yield [34]. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the SCOD content of ADCQD, ADNCQD, and ADPre-CQD was lower than the previous day from day 1 to day 5, while the daily CH4 yield continued to increase. Microorganisms consumed a large amount of organic acids and other dissolved organic matter, resulting in a reduction in SCOD content. However, on the 3rd day, the SCOD content in the AD group increased to 15,700 mg/L, which was inconsistent with the performance of the AD group with different CQD. At this point, the microbes in the system could not timely consume the soluble organic matter in the absence of quantum dots. Therefore, this led to an increase in the residual SCOD content in the system. The SCOD content of the three experimental groups (ADCQD, ADNCQD and ADPre-CQD) started to increase on day 7 and reached peak values on day 11, which were 1050 mg/L, 1544 mg/L and 1436 mg/L, respectively. Compared to previous results, the daily CH4 yield showed peaks on the 5th and 12th days (Fig. 3b), corresponding to the troughs in SCOD content. At the end of the period, the SCOD in all high-load AD systems decreased to 3330–4560 mg/L. In the ADPre-CQD system, the SCOD content was reduced by 4.4 times.

In high-load AD system, the ammonia nitrogen content is usually high. Although ammonia nitrogen is essential for microbial growth and provides some buffering capacity to the system to help maintain pH balance. Excessive amounts, however, can inhibit microbial activity [35,36]. Free ammonia (NH3) crosses the bacterial cell membrane by diffusion, causing an imbalance in the intracellular H+ concentration, or combines with external H+ to form NH4 +, altering the intracellular pH [37]. The nitrogen content of excess sludge and food waste was 1.30 % and 0.36 %, respectively (Table S5). The analysis found that the NH4 +-N concentrations in the experimental groups and the control group peaked on day 15 (Fig. 5b) and subsequently fluctuated within the range of 1000–2300 mg/L.

The change in ammonia nitrogen content in the experimental groups were more pronounced, especially in ADNCQD and ADPre-CQD (Fig. 5b), indicating more active microbial metabolism of ammonia nitrogen. In contrast, the ammonia nitrogen content in the AD group showed relatively stable changes. This indicated that ammonia nitrogen has not been consumed in a timely manner, and the ability of hydrolytic bacteria to decompose the substrate was inhibited. It was worth noting that even at higher levels of ammonia nitrogen concentration (day 20–30, 1741.9–1957.8 mg/L), the CH4 yield performance of ADPre-CQD and ADNCQD remained stable (Fig. 3). These results indicated that these two groups were not inhibited by ammonia nitrogen. It is noteworthy that the CH4 yield performance of the ADPre-CQD group remained optimal even at higher ammonia levels. The reasons for mitigating ammonia nitrogen inhibition might be attributed to the following two aspects. Firstly, Pre-CQD with smaller particle size and uniform dispersion can adhere to microbial surfaces and diffuse into the interior of cells, enhancing their electron transfer ability. Second, the surface of Pre-CQD, enriched with negatively charged functional groups such as C-O-C and C-O-H, enabled it to bind with NH4 + inside and outside the cells through diffusion and electrostatic attraction, effectively shielding ammonia nitrogen inhibition. Pretreatment enhanced these two potential effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variation of organic components in high-load AD with different CQD
ce:section.ce:para: To further analyze the effects of different CQD on substrate composition changes in high-load AD systems, the DOM components in the digestate at different digestion stages were analyzed using 3D-EEM spectroscopy [38]. In the study, the fluorescence spectra obtained from the 3D-EEM were divided into five regions based on their excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 6 . Regions I and Ⅱ represent aromatic proteins, mainly including tryptophan and tyrosine, etc. Region Ⅲ is difficult-to-degraded fulvic acids substances. Region Ⅳ is soluble microbial by-products. Region V is difficult-to-degraded humic acid-like substances [39]. The DOM of the initial digestate was mainly distributed in IV, with high-intensity peaks observed at Em/Ex = 344 ∼ 408/227 ∼ 300 and Em/Ex = 414 ∼ 444/342 ∼ 380 (Fig. 6a-d). The fluorescence peak of region Ⅳ gradually decreased with the increase of CH4 yield, indicating that the soluble microbial by-product-like substances were degraded and probably consumed by microorganisms as a nutrient source. On day 3, the strong fluorescence peaks were shifted from region Ⅳ to region V. Region Ⅳ of the AD group still retained the obvious characteristic peaks, while the experimental groups did not have characteristic fluorescence peaks in this region. This indicated that the addition of different CQD can effectively accelerate the degradation of the substrate and thus improve the gas production performance, with the most significant effect in the ADPre-CQD group (Fig. S5). On the 19th day, the area and intensity of peak at region IV decreased, which was related to the consumption of DOM by microorganisms (Fig. S5). On the 36th day, the fluorescence peak intensity in region V increased, which may be attributed to the recombination of small organic molecules into complex large molecules that were difficult to degrade in the system [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical performance of the AD with different CQD
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the ability of different CQD to promote the electron transfer during AD was investigated. Fig. 7 a showed AD group had no obvious redox peaks. Compared with the control group, the experimental groups all showed more distinct redox peaks, with the ADPre-CQD group being the most significant. The CV curves of the ADPre-CQD and ADNCQD groups observed a distinct oxidation peak around 0.6 mV, with the oxidation currents were 0.64 mA and 0.41 mA, respectively. The CV curves of the ADCQD group observed a distinct oxidation peak at 0.5 mV, with the oxidation currents was 0.32 mA. The CV curves of the ADPre-CQD group observed a distinct reduction peak around −0.27 mV, and the reduction current was −0.63 mA. The CV curves of the ADCQD group observed a distinct reduction peak around −0.24 mV, and the reduction currents were −0.47 mA. The area of CV curve represents the electron storage capacity of the system, and a large area indicates that the system has a higher specific capacitance [41]. The area of the CV curves for three experimental groups were larger than that of the control AD, indicating that the addition of different CQD has endowed the system with higher specific capacitance. It has been shown that the introduction of oxygen-containing functional groups and nitrogen can provide additional redox pseudo capacitance to carbon materials, thereby increasing their activity and energy density [42]. As shown in Fig. 2, Pre-CQD contained a large number of C=O, C-O-H, and C-O-C, and the increase in the number and strength of these functional groups effectively improved the specific capacitance of ADPre-CQD. Meanwhile, the doping of nitrogen also promoted the improvement of the specific capacitance of the ADNCQD group [43]. Additionally, the addition of different CQD significantly reduced the charge transfer resistance (Fig. 7b), which may be due to the high conductivity of these CQD. In summary, CQD provides additional electron storage capacity for the AD system and offers a sufficient conductive medium for redox reactions among microorganisms. Therefore, the electron transfer efficiency in the AD system has been improved. As shown in Fig. 7c, it was evident that the conductivity of the experimental groups was higher than that of the control group, which was consistent with the results of the CV and EIS analyses. Based on the above, the introduction of different CQD (especially NCQD and Pre-CQD) not only increased the electronic storage capacity of AD systems but also reduced charge transfer resistance and enhanced conductivity. These findings provide crucial theoretical for optimizing the design and operation of AD systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbial community structure and its correlation with performance parameters
ce:section.ce:para: The samples from the stationary phase were used to analyzed microbial community structure. The Chao index of all samples was around 1000, indicating that the biomass in the systems was abundant (Table S6). Especially in the experimental groups containing quantum dots, the Chao index showed a significant increase. Additionally, the Shannon index has also increased, indicating an enhancement in community diversity. 249 shared operational taxonomic units (OTUs) were observed in the bacterial community and 12 shared OTUs in the archaeal community (Fig. S6). In ADCQD, ADNCQD, and ADPre-CQD, the unique number of bacterial OTUs increased. Unique archaea OTUs have been discovered in ADNCQD and ADPre-CQD. The changes in OTUs of bacterial and archaeal communities indicated that the microbial community structure has been altered under the influence of different CQD.

Clostridium was able to break down carbohydrates and proteins in the substrate into micromolecule organic acids, which created the conditions for the methanogenic process [44]. It has been shown that the conductive materials can effectively enrich electroactive microorganisms in the genus Clostridia [45]. Compared with the control group, the abundance of Clostridium_sensu_stricto_1 was significantly increased by 1.36 to 1.91 times in the three experimental groups (Fig. 8 a). Bacteroidia, as hydrolytic-acidification bacteria, were able to metabolize carbohydrates using organic substrate or protons as terminal electron acceptor, thus promoting substrate hydrolysis [46]. The abundance of Bacteroidetes_vadinHA17 was up-regulated in the three experimental groups, especially in the ADPre-CQD group, where its abundance increased by 105.81 % compared to the control group. The hydrolysis and acidification process was accelerated due to the increase in microbial abundance, which provided more nutritional sources for methanogen. In addition, the abundance of the dominant bacterial community was evenly distributed among the three experimental groups, indicating a stable and coordinated operation of the AD system. It ensured that the hydrolysis, acidification and methanogenesis of AD were carried out smoothly. The addition of different CQD not only promoted the hydrolysis-acidification process, but also improved the overall performance and stability of the high-load AD system by influencing the microbial community structure.

At the genus level (Fig. 8b), the abundance of the dominant archaea Methanosarcina was up-regulated in experimental groups with different CQD. Compared with the control group, the abundance of Methanosarcina in the ADPre-CQD, ADNCQD, and ADCQD group increased by 38.56 %, 20.93 %, and 53.07 %, respectively. Methanosarcina possessed a large specific surface area and quantum dots were more easily enriched on its surface [47]. The unique surface structure was closely related to electron transfer. Multiple studies have found that Methanosarcina can form DIET by constructing electron channels with bacteria [48]. During AD, the increase in abundance of Methanosarcina significantly promoted the yield of CH4. Furthermore, in the ADPre-CQD group, the abundance of the dominant groups Methanosaeta (28.47 %), Methanobacterium (35.90 %), and Methanosarcina (20.52 %) was more evenly distributed, and the mutual synergism among different strains was enhanced [49].

Comprehensive analysis of the relationship between microbial community structure, environmental factors, and CH4 yield can provide a theoretical basis for understanding the impacts of different CQD on high-load systems. As shown in Fig. 9 , mantel test illustrated the correlation at the gene level between microbial community structure, environmental factors, and CH4 yield. Hydrogenotrophic methanogens (Methanobacterium) and acetotrophic methanogens (Methanosarcina, Methanosaeta) were negatively correlated in the network, suggesting a competitive relationship between hydrogenotrophic methanogens and acetotrophic methanogens [50]. This competitive relationship between different nutritional types has also been confirmed in previous studies [51]. Methanosarcina, Clostridium_sensu_stricto_1 and norank__f__Bacteroidetes_vadinHA17 were significantly correlated with SCOD, pH, VFA and cumulative CH4 yield. The results of the Mantel test between Romboutsia and conductivity showed that Mantel’s p ranged between 0.01–0.05 and Mantel’s r ≥ 0.04, indicating a significant positive correlation between them. Compared with the AD group, the abundance of ADPre-CQD, ADNCQD and ADCQD groups increased by 43.46 %, 58.22 % and 39.24 %, respectively. Romboutsia played a crucial role in the metabolism of short-chain fatty acids, which were metabolites of key nutrients such as carbohydrates and fats [52]. Furthermore, Romboutsia promoted the construction of the electron transfer network in AD systems by secreting various molecules that interact with other bacteria, especially electroactive bacteria. This behavior significantly enhanced the efficiency of electron transfer. We have found that the addition of different CQD increased the cumulative CH4 yield by 6 % to 18 %, the abundance of the dominant strain Methanosarcina by 38 % to 53 %, and the abundance of Clostridium_sensu_stricto_1 and norank_f__Bacteroidetes_vadinHA17 also increased significantly. In conclusion, SCOD, pH, and VFA were significant factors influencing methanogenesis in high-load AD systems. Addition of different CQD enriched the dominant strains Methanosarcina, Clostridium_sensu_stricto_1 and norank_f__Bacteroidetes_vadinHA17 to enhance AD performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The potential mechanism for promoting methanogenesis

ce:section.ce:para: The pretreatment of straw with H2O2 and acetic acid can efficiently increase the yield of straw-derived CQD to ∼40 %. In a high-loaded AD system with food waste and excess sludge as the co-substrate, the addition of different CQD could significantly enhance CH4 yield performance, with a cumulative CH4 yield increase of 6 %–18 %. The CQD synthesized from pretreated straw and the nitrogen-doped CQD synthesized using Chlorella as the nitrogen source demonstrated competitive enhancing performance for cumulative CH4 yield, with increases of 17.71 % and 8.87 %, respectively. Straw-derived CQD can increase the abundance of the dominant strains of the hydrolytic acid-producing bacteria (Clostridium_sensu_stricto_1 and Bacteroidetes_vadinHA17) and Methanosarcina, which strengthened the hydrolytic, acidification and methanogenesis phases. Electrochemical, the correlation analysis between microorganisms and performance parameters showed that the CQD established an electron conductive network to enhance the electron transfer of the system. This was a potential reason for the improvement of organic components (DOM, SCOD, and VFA) conversion and methanogenesis in high-load AD systems. These findings provide novel perspectives for optimizing AD process.
